sourceTitle: How to build a wiki for your company

questions.question: What is the primary reason the article gives for building a company wiki?

options.text: To create a decorative website for the company
options.explanation: A wiki is functional, not decorative.

options.text: To centralize information so employees can find it quickly
options.explanation: The article emphasizes that a wiki consolidates essential information and saves time.

options.text: To replace all email communication
options.explanation: While it reduces certain emails, replacing all email is not the main goal.

options.text: To increase the number of pages in Notion
options.explanation: Quantity of pages isn’t the purpose; organization and accessibility are.

questions.question: Which Notion command is used to create a new sub‑page inside an existing wiki page?

options.text: /table
options.explanation: /table creates a table, not a page.

options.text: /page
options.explanation: Typing /page and pressing Enter creates a sub‑page.

options.text: /embed
options.explanation: /embed inserts external content, not a new page.

options.text: /divider
options.explanation: /divider adds a visual line, not a page.

questions.question: When setting permissions, what does the article suggest you do if only a few people should see a certain page?

options.text: Delete the page after they read it
options.explanation: Deleting loses the information; permissions are the proper method.

options.text: Move the page to the PRIVATE section and share it with a specific group
options.explanation: The article advises placing private content in the PRIVATE sidebar and sharing with a group.

options.text: Make the whole wiki public and trust users not to look at it
options.explanation: Public access defeats the purpose of restricting visibility.

options.text: Rename the page with a secret code
options.explanation: Renaming does not control access.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a way to organize content within a Notion wiki?

options.text: Using headers (H1, H2, H3)
options.explanation: Headers are explicitly recommended for organization.

options.text: Creating columns via drag‑and‑drop
options.explanation: Columns are suggested as a clean layout method.

options.text: Adding divider lines with three hyphens
options.explanation: Dividers are mentioned for visual separation.

options.text: Applying color‑coded tags to pages
options.explanation: The article does not discuss color‑coded tags for organization.

questions.question: How does the article recommend you embed a map showing the office location?

options.text: Upload a screenshot of the map
options.explanation: Uploading an image is not the recommended dynamic method.

options.text: Type /map and paste the Google Maps URL
options.explanation: The article states you can embed a map by typing /map and pasting the URL.

options.text: Use the /embed command with a PDF of the map
options.explanation: /embed works for many files, but the article specifies /map for live maps.

options.text: Create a linked database of addresses
options.explanation: A linked database is not mentioned for map embedding.

questions.question: What advantage does the article claim a Notion wiki has over more rigid tools when it comes to customizing structure?

options.text: Notion automatically generates a table of contents
options.explanation: While Notion can generate a TOC, the article focuses on flexibility, not automation.

options.text: It allows you to treat the wiki like malleable clay, adjusting headers, columns, and pages freely
options.explanation: The article uses the clay metaphor to highlight Notion’s flexibility.

options.text: It locks the layout after the first design, ensuring consistency
options.explanation: Locking is optional and not presented as an advantage for customization.

options.text: It forces a single‑column layout for simplicity
options.explanation: Notion actually supports multiple columns, not a forced single column.

questions.question: If you want only the marketing team to edit their own wiki page while everyone else can only view it, which sequence of steps is correct?

options.text: Set workspace access to ‘Can edit’, then remove marketing from the group
options.explanation: This would give everyone edit rights, opposite of the goal.

options.text: Set workspace access to ‘Can read’, create a marketing group, and grant that group ‘Can edit’ on the page
options.explanation: The article describes exactly this workflow.

options.text: Make the page public and rely on the public link for marketing only
options.explanation: Public pages are viewable by anyone with the link, not restricted to a team.

options.text: Lock the page after creating it
options.explanation: Locking prevents all edits, not just for non‑marketing members.

questions.question: Which feature does the article suggest for creating an employee directory that includes photos and personalized info?

options.text: A standard table database with text fields only
options.explanation: Tables can hold data but the article recommends a gallery view for richer visual info.

options.text: A gallery database, customizing cards to show photos and details
options.explanation: The article explicitly recommends using a gallery for the directory.

options.text: Embedding a third‑party HR system via an iFrame
options.explanation: Embedding isn’t mentioned for the directory; the focus is on Notion’s native gallery.

options.text: Creating separate pages for each employee and linking them manually
options.explanation: While possible, the article highlights the gallery as the streamlined solution.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the ‘Page lock’ feature as described in the guide?

options.text: To encrypt the page content for security compliance
options.explanation: Locking doesn’t encrypt; it only prevents edits.

options.text: To prevent accidental edits while still allowing others to view or comment
options.explanation: The article states that locking stops accidental edits.

options.text: To automatically archive the page after 30 days
options.explanation: Archiving isn’t part of the lock function.

options.text: To hide the page from all users except the creator
options.explanation: Locking doesn’t affect visibility; it only restricts editing.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes how the article suggests you handle frequently updated policy documents?

options.text: Create a new page for each version and link them manually
options.explanation: Versioning by separate pages adds clutter; the article promotes a single living document.

options.text: Keep a single canonical page that everyone accesses, updating it in place
options.explanation: The guide emphasizes that a wiki allows updates in one place, reducing confusion.

options.text: Store PDFs in a separate file‑sharing service and link them
options.explanation: While PDFs can be embedded, the primary advice is to maintain an up‑to‑date Notion page.

options.text: Send out email newsletters whenever a policy changes
options.explanation: Email notifications are not presented as the central method for policy updates.
